1: Leviticus is the central book of the Torah and the most liturgical: it contains the instructions for the five primary offerings (burnt, grain, peace, sin, guilt), the ordination of the Aaronic priesthood, the purity laws, the Holiness Code (chs. 17-26), and the festival calendar. Its context is the completed tabernacle (Exod 40) — YHWH's glory has filled the structure, and now YHWH speaks from within it (Lev 1:1: YHWH called to Moses and spoke to him from the tent of meeting). The book addresses the central problem of how a holy God can dwell among a sinful people — the answer is a complex system of sacrifice, priesthood, purification, and consecration. Hebrews reads Leviticus as the most detailed typological preparation for Christ's high-priestly work.
2: The five major offerings introduced across chapters 1-7: (1) the burnt offering (olah, chs. 1, 6:8-13), (2) the grain offering (minḥah, chs. 2, 6:14-23), (3) the fellowship offering (shelamim, chs. 3, 7:11-36), (4) the sin offering (ḥatta't, chs. 4-5:13, 6:24-30), (5) the guilt offering (asham, chs. 5:14-6:7, 7:1-10). Chapters 1-3 introduce the voluntary offerings; chapters 4-5 introduce the obligatory reparatory offerings. Sacrificial systems were universal in the ancient Near East — Mesopotamian (Sumerian and Akkadian texts), Ugaritic, and Egyptian systems all involved animal sacrifice, grain offerings, and blood rituals. What distinguishes Israel's system is its monolatrous structure (one altar, one authorized priesthood, one deity), its emphasis on atonement theology, and its explicit grounding in covenant.
3: The olah (burnt offering) is the most common offering in the OT. Its defining characteristic is total consumption: unlike other offerings where portions belong to the priest or offerer, the entire animal is burned on the altar — hence the LXX rendering holokautoma (whole-burnt). The animal must be from the herd (baqar) — domesticated cattle — and must be a male (zakhar) without defect (tamim, perfect/complete). The presentation at the entrance to the tent of meeting (petaḥ ohel mo'ed) makes the offering a public covenant act, not a private transaction.
4: The offerer — not the priest — performs the semikah (laying of hands) and the slaughter. This is a crucial contextual point often missed: in Israel's system, the layperson killed the animal; the priest handled the sacred elements (blood, fire, altar). This distribution of roles differs from some ANE systems where priests controlled all aspects of sacrifice. The offerer's personal act of slaughter makes the sacrifice more intimate — he kills his own animal; the death is not bureaucratically managed. The laying of hands transfers identification: the animal represents the offerer before YHWH.
5: The blood-sprinkling (zaraq) is the priestly act par excellence: Aaron's sons throw the blood against all sides of the altar. The Mesopotamian parallels help clarify what blood-manipulation meant in the ancient world: blood was understood as the carrier of life, and its manipulation at the sacred space was the mechanism of connection between the human and divine realms. In Israel's system, the specific theology is provided by Lev 17:11 — blood makes atonement because life is in it. The north side of the altar is specified for certain offerings (v. 11), reflecting a consistent spatial organization of the sanctuary courts.
6: The skinning and dismemberment of the animal is performed by the offerer. The skin of burnt offerings belonged to the priest (Lev 7:8) — a practical priestly income even when the offerer received no portion of the flesh. The careful dismemberment (head, fat, internal organs, legs) reflects a systematic process ensuring complete consumption on the altar. Archaeological evidence from ancient Israelite sanctuaries (Arad, Tel Dan) shows altar structures consistent with this burning protocol.
7: The priests are responsible for the altar fire — they light it and maintain it. The fire on the altar was to burn continuously (Lev 6:12-13 will specify this explicitly). In the ancient world, sacred fires were maintained at major temples as signs of divine presence; Egypt's temples had perpetual lamps; Rome's Vestal virgins maintained the sacred fire. Israel's altar fire functions similarly but is grounded in the Sinai revelation rather than civic religion.
8: The careful arrangement of pieces (arak) on the altar is a priestly responsibility. The precision reflects the sanctuary's organization: nothing is thrown on haphazardly; the entire process is ordered and intentional. The head and fat are specifically mentioned — the head represents the whole; the fat (ḥelev) is reserved for YHWH (Lev 3:16-17). The systematic arrangement of dismembered animals on the altar was likely visible from outside the sanctuary court and would have communicated the seriousness and structure of Israel's worship.
9: The washing of internal organs and legs before burning is a purification step — removing dung and digestive contents. This practical measure also signals that even the most basic aspects of sacrifice are performed with care. The phrase reiaḥ niḥoaḥ laYHWH (pleasing aroma to YHWH) is the standard formula for accepted offerings and occurs 43 times in the Pentateuch. It is anthropomorphic language for divine acceptance — not that YHWH literally smells, but that the offering is received with pleasure. Paul applies this language to Christ's offering in Eph 5:2 and to the Philippians' gift in Phil 4:18.
10: The graduated accessibility of the burnt offering: bull (v. 3) → sheep or goat (v. 10) → bird (v. 14). This descending scale democratizes access to YHWH — the wealthy bring bulls; the middle class bring sheep or goats; the poor bring birds. The same graduated scale appears in the sin offering (ch. 4-5), the cleansing of skin diseases (Lev 14), and post-birth purification (Lev 12:8). Mary and Joseph bring two birds at Jesus's presentation (Luke 2:24), indicating their household's economic status.
11: The north side of the altar is specified for the flock offering — consistent with the spatial organization of the court. The north side was the designated slaughter area for certain offerings, probably to keep the blood flow away from the sanctuary entrance on the east side. The altar's geography organized Israel's sacrificial activity spatially, turning the court into a purposefully arranged liturgical space.
12: The same dismemberment procedure for flock animals as for cattle — consistent treatment regardless of the animal's size. The systematic approach emphasizes that the offering's value lies not in its monetary worth but in its proper presentation. A goat properly offered is as accepted as a bull properly offered.
13: The washing of entrails and legs applies to all burnt offerings. The formula reiaḥ niḥoaḥ laYHWH closes each offering type — the aroma language signals that the offering has achieved its intended purpose of pleasing YHWH. In ANE temple texts, gods were described as smelling sacrifices favorably; the Gilgamesh Epic (tablet XI) describes the gods gathering like flies around Utnapishtim's sacrifice after the flood. Israel uses the same language but locates it within a covenantal rather than polytheistic framework.
14: The bird offering: turtledove (tor) or young pigeon (ben-yonah). Birds were the minimum viable sacrifice — accessible to the very poor. Their smaller size explains the modified procedure that follows: no dismemberment, different blood disposal. The specification of two species reflects the practical availability of different birds in the Levant — both were common, nest near human habitation, and could be obtained without significant expense.
15: The priest performs all acts for bird offerings — unlike cattle and flock offerings where the offerer participates. This reflects the bird's smaller scale: there is no skin, and the blood must be drained at the altar side rather than thrown in quantity. The wringing of the neck (rather than throat-cutting) is the standard method for small birds. The blood drain at the side of the altar achieves the same theological purpose as the sprinkling in larger offerings.
16: The crop and feathers are removed and disposed of in the ash heap on the east side of the altar. The ash heap (deshen) was a designated disposal area for sacrificial refuse — it was clean enough to be near the sanctuary but separated from the altar itself. The east side facing the entrance — the direction from which worshippers approached — was where the ash was deposited, perhaps as a visible sign of ongoing sacrificial activity.
17: The bird is split at the wings without complete division — it is presented as a whole unit spread open on the altar. The partial splitting exposes the interior for complete burning while maintaining the bird's wholeness. The closing formula reiaḥ niḥoaḥ laYHWH confirms that the bird offering is equally accepted as the bull offering — economic inequality does not create liturgical inequality before YHWH.
